title: Islamic manuscript binding: structure and history
subtitle: The envelope-flap cover, tacketed sewing, and pasteboards of the Arabic and Persian bookbinding tradition
excerpt: Islamic manuscript binding developed independently of the European codex tradition. Its distinctive features include a foredge envelope flap, a tacketed (laced-on) sewing structure, and pasteboards made from layers of laminated paper rather than the wooden boards of the West.
sourceNotes: Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907), chapters on Eastern bindings. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookitshistoryan00dave. Davenport describes the foredge flap and pasteboard structure. Also: descriptions of Quranic binding structures in period Islamic manuscript-library catalogues available through the British Library digitised collections.

glossaryTerms.definition: The triangular extension of the back cover of an Islamic binding that folds over the foredge of the book to protect the text block when the book is stored horizontally on a shelf.
A short loop of thread or leather that laces the sewing supports to the cover boards from the outside, rather than gluing or lacing through the board thickness as in the Western tradition.

A bookbinding board made by laminating many layers of paper together with paste, rather than pressing a single sheet of board stock. Islamic pasteboards are lighter than the European binders board they resemble.
The tongue-and-slot fastening on an Islamic binding's envelope flap, which tucks under the front cover board to hold the book closed.

content.text: The European and Islamic bookbinding traditions developed side by side for roughly a thousand years and influenced each other at points of contact, particularly in Spain and Sicily. But the structural decisions they made are markedly different. Where a European case binding laces sewing supports through holes in a thick wooden or binders board, an Islamic binding uses laminated
pasteboards
and a
tacketed
sewing attachment. Where a European book stands upright on a shelf spine-out, an Islamic book traditionally lies flat spine-inward, protected by its
envelope flap
. The differences are not superficial. They reflect different assumptions about how a book should behave, how long it should last, and what a bound object is.
The pasteboard

Islamic binding boards are built up from layers of paper pasted together in a mould. Sheet after sheet of worn manuscript paper, discarded pages, or freshly made paper is layered and compressed until the laminate reaches the required thickness. Davenport describes boards of twenty or more layers in some Quranic bindings.

The result is lighter than a European binders board of the same thickness. It is more flexible when damp. Under heat, it can be shaped into the slight curve that many Islamic bindings show in the cover boards. The pasteboard is also reversible: a conservator can separate the pasted layers.
The tacketed sewing
Islamic manuscripts are typically sewn through the signatures in a simple kettle-stitch-like structure, but the boards are not attached by lacing sewing supports through the board. Instead, short
tackets

of thread or leather loop through holes near the spine edge of each board. The tacket passes through the board from the outside, picks up a loop of the sewing thread, and returns to the outside where it is knotted.

This means the boards are attached through the board face rather than through the board thickness. The result opens and closes differently from a European book. The hinge is at the surface, not at the spine shoulder; the book flexes at the spine edge of the board rather than at a groove.
The envelope flap and iqlid
The back cover of an Islamic binding extends beyond the foredge of the book as a triangular flap. This flap, the
envelope flap
, folds over the foredge when the book is closed to protect the pages from dust and handling damage. The tip of the flap carries an
iqlid
, a small tongue that tucks under the front cover board to hold the assembly shut without a clasp or tie.

The flap is an integral part of the cover: it is cut from the same piece of leather or cloth as the back board and spine covering, not added separately. This means the cover is a single continuous piece that wraps the spine, the back board, and the protective flap in one.
Decoration

Islamic binding decoration peaks in the 15th and 16th centuries in Persia and the Mamluk tradition. Covers are tooled in gold, blocked with geometric patterns, and inlaid with coloured leather. The central medallion and corner-piece layout seen on Persian bindings reached European bookbinders through Spain and Italy.

Some high-quality bindings add a lacquered surface over the covered boards. The doublure, the inner face of the cover board, is painted and lacquered in floral or figural designs. This technique is the ancestor of Japanese lacquered bindings. It has no equivalent in the European tradition.
A worked example: the Quranic binding

A Quranic binding from the Mamluk period (13th to 16th centuries) typically has: a pasteboard board of 3 to 5 mm, built from up to thirty laminated layers; a tacketed attachment at the spine edge, visible as small thread or leather loops; full leather tooled in a geometric repeat; a foredge envelope flap with an iqlid tip; and a painted and gilded doublure inside each board. The spine does not show. The book lies flat with the spine against the shelf.
Relevance for contemporary binders

The tacketed attachment and envelope flap are both achievable for a home binder. Tacketed bindings need no press: the boards attach with looped thread. The envelope flap can be cut from heavy paper or a piece of leather covering, adding a practical element to any long-stitch or pamphlet book. The structure is documented in Davenport and visible in photographs in the British Library and Bibliothèque nationale de France digitised collections.
